narrative: As a Black male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background and plans to attend law school full-time, your profile stood out in several ways, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.90 and LSAT score of 33.50, which are below the typical averages of 3.21 and 36.70 seen among past students in our dataset. The admissions model, trained on records of students' academic paths and bar exam outcomes, predicted a 60.5% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try—a threshold that led to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes candidates likely to succeed and pass promptly. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, placing you in the bottom tenth of your peers that year, which strongly signaled potential challenges ahead compared to typical students who ranked much higher around the middle of their classes. Next in importance was your LSAT score, which actually worked in your favor by nudging the prediction slightly toward passing, though not enough to offset other concerns since it fell short of the stronger scores most successful applicants had. Your Year 1 decile of 1 also contributed toward the failure outlook, reinforcing a pattern of lower early performance relative to the average around the fifth or sixth decile. These three academic markers—your end-of-high-school and early college rankings alongside the LSAT—dominated the model's decision, outweighing positives like your full-time commitment, which is the norm for over 90% of students. While factors like family income provided some stability, they didn't sway the core academic signals that the model weighs heavily for bar passage. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black man from a solid background pursuing law full-time; it's the model highlighting where your record diverges from those who typically pass the bar first time. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above average, strengthening your grades in any ongoing studies to climb those decile rankings, and perhaps seeking targeted bar prep resources early. Gathering stronger letters of recommendation or relevant experience could also help reframe your story in a future application. Many students in similar positions have improved key metrics and succeeded—persistence and focused improvement are key next steps for you.
